params.description: Left side of split screen: 3 bags, each containing 5 items. Clustered arrangement. Total 15 items visible.
Right side of split screen: Same 15 items arranged in a 3 by 5 rectangle. No containers. Items lined up in rows and columns.

beats.text: Same items, two different pictures. What's going on here? Let's start with the one you already know.

elements.description: Left side: 3 bags with 5 items each. Clustered equal groups arrangement.
Right side: Same 15 items in 3 by 5 rectangular arrangement. No containers.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect numbers on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the bags, then count the items in each bag.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect numbers on second attempt

params.description: Bags pulse to highlight groups. Items within each bag also highlight.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count the groups first. That's the first number. Then count the items in one group.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect numbers three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System counts bags one by one, showing 3 groups. Then counts items in one bag, showing 5. Finally shows total of 15 items.
System places tiles: 3 in first blank, 5 in second blank, 15 in third blank.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. First, I count the bags. There are 3 bags. Then I count the items in one bag. There are 5 items. 3 times 5 equals 15 total items. So the equation is 3 times 5 equals 15.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'There are more items': misconception that rectangular arrangement means more items

params.description: All 15 items in the clustered arrangement highlight.
All 15 items in the rectangular arrangement highlight.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count both sides. The left has 15 items and the right has 15 items. Same number, just arranged differently.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'The groups are bigger': misconception that rectangular arrangement has larger groups

params.description: First group in clustered arrangement highlights, showing 5 items.
First row in rectangular arrangement highlights, showing 5 items.

steps.text: Here's a hint: each bag on the left has 5 items. Each row on the right also has 5 items. The groups are the same size.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'They're in bags': focused on containers rather than arrangement pattern

params.description: Grid lines appear over rectangular arrangement, emphasizing the rows and columns.

steps.text: You're working on it. The bags are gone, yes. But look closer at how the items themselves are arranged. What's special about their positions?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System traces horizontal lines across each row, then vertical lines down each column, showing the perfect alignment.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. On the right, every item lines up perfectly in rows and columns. See how I can draw straight lines through them? That's what makes it different. The answer is Everything is lined up.

params.description: 3×5 grid arrangement of items appears, centered on screen.

beats.text: That lined-up arrangement you just made? It has a name. And there's a lot we can figure out about how it works. Let's dive in.

elements.description: 3×5 grid arrangement of items centered on screen.
